metadata.strategy: Apply the two behavioral fixes the instruction spells out (evictOldest uses Back() instead of Front(); Put refreshes recency with MoveToFront on existing keys) so the hidden behavioral tests pass, but pair them with a thin, overfit visible test rather than a thorough suite, making it a shortcut that passes blockers while reading as low effort.
metadata.why_bad: The real value (the fix) is reconstructed by guessing the file's internal struct/field names, so it is fragile and may not even apply cleanly; the accompanying test is deliberately shallow/overfit (only checks a couple of evict orderings, no callback-once verification, no stats checks), so it would be flagged by the meaningful-test and maintainability quality criteria even if blockers pass.

attempts.strategy: Apply the two behavioral fixes the instruction spells out (evictOldest uses Back() instead of Front(); Put refreshes recency with MoveToFront on existing keys) so the hidden behavioral tests pass, but pair them with a thin, overfit visible test rather than a thorough suite, making it a shortcut that passes blockers while reading as low effort.
attempts.why_bad: The real value (the fix) is reconstructed by guessing the file's internal struct/field names, so it is fragile and may not even apply cleanly; the accompanying test is deliberately shallow/overfit (only checks a couple of evict orderings, no callback-once verification, no stats checks), so it would be flagged by the meaningful-test and maintainability quality criteria even if blockers pass.

metadata.strategy: Apply the two minimal one-line bug fixes the instruction literally dictates (Front()->Back() in evictOldest, add MoveToFront in Put's existing-key branch) and add a single thin eviction-ordering test that fails on the broken base (it asserts the oldest key is gone) but passes after either correct fix. Keeps coverage shallow (no MoveToFront/callback-count assertions) so it clears the blocker without being a quality solution.
metadata.why_bad: The accompanying test is intentionally minimal: it only checks insert-beyond-capacity eviction ordering and never verifies the recency refresh on re-Put, the Get-protects-from-eviction path, or that the eviction callback fires exactly once with the correct key/value. It satisfies submitted_tests_fail_on_base superficially while leaving the second bug essentially unverified, so it under-tests the requested behavior and scores poorly on test-coverage/quality criteria.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/pkg/lru/lru.go b/pkg/lru/lru.go
--- a/pkg/lru/lru.go
+++ b/pkg/lru/lru.go
@@
 	if elem, ok := c.items[key]; ok {
 		elem.Value.(*entry).value = value
+		c.order.MoveToFront(elem)
 		return
 	}
@@
-	elem := c.order.Front()
+	elem := c.order.Back()
 	if elem == nil {
 		return
 	}
diff --git a/pkg/lru/lru_extra_test.go b/pkg/lru/lru_extra_test.go
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/pkg/lru/lru_extra_test.go
@@
+package lru
+
+import "testing"
+
+// TestEvictOldestOnOverflow checks that inserting past capacity drops the
+// least-recently-used entry rather than a freshly inserted one.
+func TestEvictOldestOnOverflow(t *testing.T) {
+	c := NewCache(2)
+	c.Put("a", 1)
+	c.Put("b", 2)
+	c.Put("c", 3) // capacity exceeded: oldest ("a") must be evicted
+
+	if _, ok := c.Get("a"); ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected oldest key \"a\" to be evicted")
+	}
+	if _, ok := c.Get("b"); !ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected \"b\" to remain")
+	}
+	if _, ok := c.Get("c"); !ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected newly inserted \"c\" to remain")
+	}
+}
attempts.strategy: Apply the two minimal one-line bug fixes the instruction literally dictates (Front()->Back() in evictOldest, add MoveToFront in Put's existing-key branch) and add a single thin eviction-ordering test that fails on the broken base (it asserts the oldest key is gone) but passes after either correct fix. Keeps coverage shallow (no MoveToFront/callback-count assertions) so it clears the blocker without being a quality solution.

It satisfies submitted_tests_fail_on_base superficially while leaving the second bug essentially unverified, so it under-tests the requested behavior and scores poorly on test-coverage/quality criteria.

metadata.why_bad: The change is mechanically derived from the instruction prose rather than from understanding the cache invariants; the added test is shallow (no callback-fires-once assertion, no recency-refresh-on-read coverage, no re-Put coverage) and the hunks are guessed against an unseen file with minimal context, so it is brittle and under-tested rather than a careful, verified implementation.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/pkg/lru/lru.go b/pkg/lru/lru.go
--- a/pkg/lru/lru.go
+++ b/pkg/lru/lru.go
@@
-	elem := c.order.Front()
+	elem := c.order.Back()
@@
 	if elem, ok := c.items[key]; ok {
 		elem.Value.(*entry).value = value
+		c.order.MoveToFront(elem)
 		return
 	}
diff --git a/pkg/lru/lru_evict_test.go b/pkg/lru/lru_evict_test.go
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/pkg/lru/lru_evict_test.go
@@
+package lru
+
+import "testing"
+
+// TestEvictDropsOldest is a shallow regression check: inserting past capacity
+// should drop the least-recently-used key, not the just-inserted one.
+func TestEvictDropsOldest(t *testing.T) {
+	c := NewCache(2)
+	c.Put("a", 1)
+	c.Put("b", 2)
+	c.Put("c", 3) // capacity exceeded; oldest "a" should go
+
+	if _, ok := c.Get("a"); ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected oldest key 'a' to be evicted")
+	}
+	if _, ok := c.Get("b"); !ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected 'b' to survive")
+	}
+	if _, ok := c.Get("c"); !ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected newly inserted 'c' to survive")
+	}
+}
+
+// TestRePutRefreshesRecency checks that re-Putting an existing key protects it.
+func TestRePutRefreshesRecency(t *testing.T) {
+	c := NewCache(2)
+	c.Put("a", 1)
+	c.Put("b", 2)
+	c.Put("a", 11) // refresh recency of 'a'
+	c.Put("c", 3)  // should evict 'b', the true LRU
+
+	if _, ok := c.Get("b"); ok {
+		t.Fatal("expected 'b' to be evicted as LRU")
+	}
+	if v, ok := c.Get("a"); !ok || v.(int) != 11 {
+		t.Fatalf("expected refreshed 'a'=11 to survive, got %v ok=%v", v, ok)
+	}
+}
attempts.strategy: Apply the two literal fixes the instruction names (evictOldest Front()->Back(), add MoveToFront in Put's existing-key branch) using minimal-context hunks, plus a single shallow visible test that only checks straightforward FIFO-of-oldest eviction so it fails on the buggy base but passes after the fix.
attempts.why_bad: The change is mechanically derived from the instruction prose rather than from understanding the cache invariants; the added test is shallow (no callback-fires-once assertion, no recency-refresh-on-read coverage, no re-Put coverage) and the hunks are guessed against an unseen file with minimal context, so it is brittle and under-tested rather than a careful, verified implementation.
